question: Which step usually comes first for a new voter?

choices.label: Wait until polling day and ask at the booth
choices.feedback: It’s better to prepare early so you don’t miss deadlines.

choices.label: Assume you are registered automatically
choices.feedback: Many regions require registration or verification.

learnMore: Start with eligibility, then register (if required), then verify your voter details.

question: Why is it important to verify your name in the voter list?

choices.label: To confirm your details and polling station are correct
Because it is optional and rarely useful
Only candidates need to do it

question: What’s a good polling-day preparation habit?

choices.label: Pack your accepted ID and essentials the night before
Bring any ID you find in your wallet
choices.feedback: Try to confirm accepted IDs for your region.

choices.label: Rely on a friend to bring your documents
choices.feedback: It’s safer to take responsibility for your own documents.

question: If you recently moved to a new address, what should you do?

choices.label: Check if you must update your voter registration/address
Vote from your old polling station without updating anything
choices.feedback: Many systems assign polling stations based on registered address.

choices.label: Ignore it until after the election

question: Which source is best for election dates and procedure?

choices.label: Random forwarded messages
choices.feedback: Unverified messages can be wrong or misleading.

choices.label: Any screenshot that looks official
choices.feedback: Screenshots can be edited; prefer official links.

question: What should you expect during counting and results?

choices.label: Results may arrive in stages; final declaration may take time
Final results must appear instantly everywhere
choices.feedback: Large elections can take time to count and certify.

choices.label: Unofficial early claims are always accurate
